coredata.dc:title: Sensing, detoxification and bactericidal applications of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Tammina, Sai Kumar
L., Jyothi
Kumar, Jothi Vinoth
Srivastava, Harshal
Naraharisetty, Sri Ram G.
coredata.dc:description: Here, a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis technique was used to create nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). Their structural and optical properties were assessed using XPS, XRD, Raman, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The N-CDs demonstrated encouraging emission quenching towards p-NP (p-Nitrophenol) and developed a sensing approach between 0.25 and 125 μM concentrations, from which a linear equation with a 20 nM detection limit was generated. In comparison to river and circulating water samples, the practicality demonstrated positive recovery rates together with impressive accuracy and precision. Additionally, the multi-functionality of N-CDs was investigated further in the catalytic reduction of methyl orange (MO) dye. With a good regression coefficient(0.9904) (R2) and rate constant (Kcat) (0.0208 min−1), N-CDs were able to reduce the azo groups in 8 min completely. Additionally, the bactericidal efficiency of N-CDs against gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus, Listeria monocytogenes) and gram-negative (Escherichia coli, Salmonella enterica) bacteria was encouraging. The well-diffusion method demonstrated a good zone of inhibition, and the microdilution method demonstrated a promising minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of 470–940 μg/mL and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 240–470 μg/mL. The suggested fluorescent material may prove useful in the future for creating a fluorescence sensor, catalysts to combat water pollution, and antibacterial sprays to combat dangerous microorganisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CDs
p-NP
Methyl orange
Reduction
Antibacterial activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Plausible mechanism for the emission quenching of N-CDs
Effect of solvent polarity on fluorescence emission of N-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Institute of Education, University of London
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: N-CDs Survey spectrum (a) C 1s spectrum (b) N 1s spectrum (c) O 1s spectrum (d).
TEM image with 20 nm resolution and Histogram of N-CDs.
(a). FT-IR (b) Raman, (c) Zeta potential and TRPL graph of N-CDs.

(a) N-CDs absorption spectrum in UV–Vis's region (b) N-CDs emission variation with excitation energies (c) Excitation and corresponding emission spectrum (Inset: N-CDs fluorescence emission under the UV lamp).
N-CDs selectivity in the presence of different interfering analytes (50 μM).
(a) Luminescence quenching of N-CDs against p-NP and (b) N-CDs linear response to p-NP (0.25–25 μM); Inset: Line graph of total concentration range (0.25–125 μM) of p-NP against N-CDs.

Absorbance changes of MO in the presence of NaBH4 (b) Absorbance changes of MO in the presence of NaBH4 and N-CDs (c) linear plot for the catalytic reduction of MO dye in the presence of N-CDs and NaBH4.
Zone of inhibition of control and N-CDs against L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, E. coli, and S. enterica.
Schematic representation of reduction of methyl orange to amine moieties.
Comparison table of N-CDs L.O.D. against p-NP as per the available literature.
Real sample analysis result, performed against circulating and river water samples (n = 3).
Zone of inhibition and MIC/MBC comparative table of N-CDs against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria.

head.ce:title: Sensing, detoxification and bactericidal applications of nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, University of Hyderabad, Telangana 500046, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, University of Hyderabad, Telangana, India-500046

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, Bio-nanocomposite Research Centre, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food and Nutrition, Bio-nanocomposite Research Centre, Kyung Hee University, Seoul-02447, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Plant Sciences, School of Life Sciences, University of Hyderabad, Telangana 50046, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Plant Sciences, School of Life Sciences, University of Hyderabad, Telangana, India-50046

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: S.K. Tammina, Department of Food and Nutrition, Bio-nanocomposite Research Centre, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis technique was used to create nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). Their structural and optical properties were assessed using XPS, XRD, Raman, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The N-CDs demonstrated encouraging emission quenching towards p-NP (p-Nitrophenol) and developed a sensing approach between 0.25 and 125 μM concentrations, from which a linear equation with a 20 nM detection limit was generated. In comparison to river and circulating water samples, the practicality demonstrated positive recovery rates together with impressive accuracy and precision. Additionally, the multi-functionality of N-CDs was investigated further in the catalytic reduction of methyl orange (MO) dye. With a good regression coefficient(0.9904) (R2) and rate constant (Kcat) (0.0208 min−1), N-CDs were able to reduce the azo groups in 8 min completely. Additionally, the bactericidal efficiency of N-CDs against gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus, Listeria monocytogenes) and gram-negative (Escherichia coli, Salmonella enterica) bacteria was encouraging. The well-diffusion method demonstrated a good zone of inhibition, and the microdilution method demonstrated a promising minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of 470–940 μg/mL and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 240–470 μg/mL. The suggested fluorescent material may prove useful in the future for creating a fluorescence sensor, catalysts to combat water pollution, and antibacterial sprays to combat dangerous microorganisms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: P-nitrophenol (p-NP) is widely employed in fine chemical synthesis processes. It is a raw addition for regularly used compounds/chemicals, including insecticides, dyes, photosensitive materials, medications, rubber additives, etc. [1]. However, long exposures to the p-NP can misbalance the central nervous system and lead to many diseases and disorders [2]. Because of this, the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) enlisted it as a prime pollutants directory [3]. Because p-NP is proven as a harmful pollutant to the health of human beings and the environment, instituting a hasty, low-cost, simple, selective, and sensitive method for determining the p-NP in environmental samples at a trace level is a very significant aspect [4].

Also, azo dyes were an essential component in industrial processes for plastic, paint, textiles, cosmetics, and printing. Nevertheless, methyl orange and other azo dyes have mutagenic and carcinogenic qualities [5]. More than 15 % of used azo dyes end up in wastewater, which seriously harms a variety of ecosystems [6]. The breakdown of such organic molecules as pre- or post-treatment using physical or chemical means is being researched for environmental safety [7]. A range of technologies, including adsorption, photocatalytic degradation, membrane filtering, electrochemical approach, and biological method, have been used to remove organic contaminants from aquatic environments [8–11]. Catalytic degradation is thought to be the most efficient and dependable strategy for treating wastewater that contains dyes. Recent research has demonstrated that the catalytic reduction of NaBH4 and catalyst can quickly eliminate a wide variety of harmful dyes. The molecules of dye are not easily and successfully purified using a standard treatment due to their high degree of stability and water solubility. The conversion of dyes into usable chemicals through catalytic reduction is an eco-friendly, straightforward, and economical process. The methylene blue compound is transformed into Leuco-methylene blue, while the orange G component is transformed into amine derivatives [12–14]. Similarly, methyl orange converts to non-toxic amine compounds through catalytic reduction [15]. An essential technique for environmental remediation, the breakdown of the dyes into innocuous and nonhazardous compounds is important from an industrial standpoint.

Meanwhile, the rise of numerous diseases, the collapse of the industrial supply, tainted drugs and food, and other problems have made bacterial infections a severe hazard to humans. Antibiotic drugs will be a key component in the fight against diseases now caused by dangerous bacterial strains. The pursuit of novel antibiotics since the creation of penicillin in 1928 has advanced medical science by shielding billions of people from bacterial diseases. However, many antibiotic medications with reduced solubility and bioavailability require their administration in higher dosages [16,17]. Antimicrobial resistance (AMR), which results from overusing antibiotics, is the development of drug-resistant bacteria. The capacity of microorganisms to persist or live even when exposed to increasing dosages of antibiotic therapy. Genes from microorganisms may naturally alter or be replaced, including genes for antibiotic resistance, to cause the process to begin [18]. Due to the rise of bacterial drug resistance, many antibiotics are ineffective at treating diseases. This might lead to protracted infection periods, expensive medication, and a rise in the mortality rate of even simple microbial infections. A key concern for healthcare companies is AMR [19–25]. According to studies, AMR is responsible for about 700,000 deaths worldwide yearly, and by 2050, that number may rise to 10 million [26,27].

Given the continued growth of AMR, low-toxic and effective antibacterial medications are critically needed by the global community to fight infectious diseases. The natural antibacterial properties of nanoparticles (NPs) are currently of great interest, and various papers assert that they can simultaneously kill gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria [28]. Because of their size, permeability, and compatibility with the microbial system, which allows them to directly attack the bacterial cell wall and demonstrate their action through various antibacterial mechanisms, these NPs are less likely to cause antimicrobial resistance than antibiotic medications [29]. However, NPs noted a few issues that must be handled for widespread bactericidal usage, including toxicity, accumulation, and expensive manufacturing techniques [30].

Bringing a common solution for different challenges is necessary to minimize the time and resources. Thus, developing a material with promising quality in a cheaper way is an emerging challenge in all situations. In contrast to other nanomaterials, CDs are naturally biocompatible and biodegradable in living things, making them less hazardous to the environment. The advantages of carbon dots among all nanomaterials are exceptional because of their inherent properties of stability, hydrophilicity, smaller size, fascinating photophysical/chemical properties, cost-effective synthesis strategies, and biocompatibility [31]. Carbon dots were applied in the vast majority of applications, including sensing [32], energy storage [33], bioimaging [34], fingerprint imaging [35], adsorption [36] and drug delivery [37]. For diverse applications, the functional groups that should be added to the surface of CDs can vary depending on the precursors, synthesis methods, and other elements. The stability, solubility, and emission properties of the resultant CDs may all be greatly enhanced. Additionally, the CDs' functional groups, surface charge, and size affect their functional properties significantly [38–41].

Here, glucosamine (GA) and ethylenediamine as nitrogen and carbon sources, using a simple hydrothermal technique to produce the N-doped CDs for p-NP detection in environmental samples, detoxification of MO dye and bactericidal testing against gram-positive & and gram-negative bacteria.

The glucosamine-hydrochloride and ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich chemicals and used without further purification. Agilent Carry 5000 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer is used for recording absorption spectra, and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using HORIBA Scientific Fluorolog Fluorescence spectrophotometer, USA. Diffraction measurements were carried out through X-ray Diffraction of PANalytical, Model: Xpert3 Powder, Japan. Zeta potential was measured using Anton Paar Litesizer 500, Austria. Raman spectrum was recorded using a 532 nm Helium-Neon laser of Horiba Jobinyvon 800, USA. An FP-8500 spectrophotometer (Jasco, Japan) was used to measure the PLQY of the N-CDs. The Quantarus-Taufluorescence lifetime measurement equipment (C11367–03, Hamamatsu Photonics Co., Japan) was used to record the fluorescence lifetime measurements. In order to identify the functional groups on the surface of N-CDs, 32 scans with a 4 cm−1 resolution were made using an IFS 66 V/S (Bruker) Fourier transforms infrared spectrometer (FTIR) in the 4000–500 cm−1 range. Using a K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Co. Ltd., Waltham, MA, USA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to assess the chemical composition of N-CDs.

We modified our previously described synthesis method [42]. A 50 mL mixture of distilled water, 1.5 g of glucosamine, and 3 mL of ethylenediamine were placed into a Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave containing the test solution was moved to a hydrothermal reactor for 4 h at 200 °C. The reaction was completed, and the autoclave was allowed to cool to ambient temperature before collecting the final solution. Following centrifugation, the solution was filtered using Whatman No. 42 filter paper. The filtered product was lyophilized to obtain the powder sample. The powder had been kept in storage for future use.

Different concentrations of p-NP solution (0.25–125 μM) were combined with 2 μL of N-CDs. This mixture was diluted with double-distilled water up to 4 mL and vortexed for a few minutes. After this mixture was cultured for a short while, the variations in emission at 459 nm were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

For the authentic sample examination, a water sample was taken from the Krishna River in Vijayawada, Andhra Pradesh, India, as well as circulating water. These two water samples were filtered via Whatman filter paper to separate the larger particles and stored for later use (No. 42). These samples were combined with the N-CDs solution. Under the optimum circumstances, they were vortexed for a while and then incubated. Variations in emission were analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer at 459 nm after the incubation, and samples were prepared in triplicates.

The reduction of Methyl Orange (MO) dyes was carried out using sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and N-CDs catalyst. 1 mL of freshly prepared ice-cold NaBH4 (0.05 M), 1.5 mL (10 ppm) of freshly prepared MO dye solution, and 0.5 mL of distilled water were added to the mixture for the catalytic reaction. To begin the catalytic reduction reaction, 80 μL of (N-CDs) was added to the reaction mixture, and the absorbance changes at linear time intervals were recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The antibacterial activity of N-CDs was determined through the well-diffusion and microdilution methods. The gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria (Salmonella enterica and Escherichia coli) and gram-positive bacteria (Listeria monocytogenes and Staphylococcus aureus) were spread on the media plates (106 CFU/mL). After spreading, the wells were made on the plates, and 100 μL of N-CD solution was added to the wells. After inoculation, the N-CDs loaded media plates were incubated at 37 °C overnight, and the zone of inhibition was measured the following day. Using the broth microdilution method, which establishes the MIC (minimum inhibitory concentration) and MBC (minimum bactericidal concentration) of CDs against the test bacteria, the bactericidal potential of CDs was assessed against common Gram-negative bacteria and Gram-positive bacteria. This was accomplished by serially diluting 1 mL of CD solution in a 96-well microtiter plate from 15 mg/mL to 0.05 mg/mL. The experiment was carried out in triplicate, and varied quantities of CDs, 100 μL of broth, and 30 μL of bacterial strain (106 CFU/mL) were introduced to each well. Overnight incubation at 37 °C was performed on the prepared plates. Redox indicator resazurin, 0.1 mg/mL, was added to 30 μL of the mixture after incubation.

By observing the XRD diffraction pattern, the generation of carbon dots can be verified (Fig. 1 ). The N-CDs demonstrated diffraction at an angle of 24.8o, a typical characteristic band of graphitic carbon, demonstrating that the synthesized CDs acquired a similarity to graphite structure [43] and confirm the formation of carbon dots.

The functional groups and elemental composition detail the surface environment of the carbon dots. The survey spectrum displayed three bands at the binding energies of 287, 410, and 533 eV, which correspond to the C 1s (66.27 %), N 1s (17.52 %), and O 1s (9.82 %), respectively (Fig. 2a). Three bands for C-C/C=C, CN, and C=O/C=N were produced by the deconvolution of the C 1s binding energy, at respective energies of 284.6, 285.7, and 286.9 eV [44] (Fig. 2b). Also, the deconvoluted N 1s spectrum displayed two bands at 399.4 & 400.9 eV, attributed to CN and NH functional groups, respectively [45] (Fig. 2c). The functional groups from O 1s deconvolution generated two binding energies of CO and C-O-C/C-OH at 531.6 and 532.5 eV, respectively [46] (Fig. 2d). This finding demonstrates how C, N, and O-rich moieties are functionalized onto N-CDs surface [45].

The TEM method was used to clarify the distribution of particle sizes in N- CDs samples (Fig. 3 ). The monodispersed, quasi-spherical shape of the CDs, without any aggregation, was shown in the HR-TEM image. Most N-CD sizes were in the size range of 2–3 nm (Fig. 3b), further confirming CD formation within the 5 nm size.

The structural information of N-CDs is elucidated with FT-IR and Raman techniques. Fig. 4 a, shows the FTIR spectrum of N-CDs, which denoted the presence of various functional groups of starting materials that were utilized for the N-CDs preparation. The stretching vibrations of amine groups may be found at 3167 and 1557 cm-1, and OH stretching vibration was observed at 3263 cm−1. Also, the vibrations at 1382 and 1151 frequencies denote the presence of CO and CN stretching vibrations. Also, C-OH group absorption was found at 1081 cm−1. The observed functional groups are in good agreement with the XPS information and indicate the existence of functional groups on the N-CDs surface. Raman is a powerful tool to identify nanomaterials' lattice disorderedness and hybridization. The N-CDs Raman spectrum exhibits two peaks at 1340 & 1572 cm−1, related to D-band and G-band, respectively (Fig. 4b). In this case, D denotes the sp3 carbon, and G denotes the sp2 carbon. The G band may correspond to the vibrations of the sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice structure, and similarly, the D band may originate from the vibrations of carbon atoms at the disordered graphite termination plane [47]. This observation further confirms the formation of N-CDs by displaying the similar hybridization of graphite molecules. The Zeta potential measurements were done to assess N-CDs surface charge and colloidal suspension stability (ζ) (Fig. 4c). The ζ accounts for the net electrical charge stored around the region bounded by a stable colloidal solution and slipping plane. This ζ value should be below 30 mV [48]. The N-CDs colloidal solutions zeta potential distribution, depicted in Fig. S1, was measured at −21 mV, demonstrating the material's promising stability in the presence of a net negative charge on its surface.

Optical properties were evaluated to find the reactivity of N-CDs with respect to the Uv-Vis radiations. Under the scanning of 200–800 nm UV–Vis radiations, two absorption bands at 278 nm and 376 nm were observed, which were attributed to the aromatic sp2 carbon atoms of n-π* and π-π* transitions, demonstrating the core conjugated structure of N-CDs (Fig. 5
a) [49]. Even if the cause of the unique character of fluorescence CDs is unclear, luminescence is also one of the most important functions of CDs from a theoretical and practical standpoint. Many methods have been proposed based on surface passivation, fluorophores, emissive trapping sites, and edge effects [50]. Surface functionalization, of all methods, is one of the most intriguing phenomena because it can provide well-organized instructions for creating fluorescent carbon dots with attractive optical qualities. Additionally, the passivation or functionalization of the surface of N-CDs, which stabilizes them, causes the radiative recombination of electrons and holes. According to the surface functionalization method, this design could offer a good photoluminescence quantum yield [42]. Fluorescence emission changes were recorded from 300 to 400 nm excitation wavelengths, where the maximum emission peak was observed at 459 nm with the excitation wavelength of 390 nm, demonstrating that the synthesized N-CDs showed the emission based on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 5b&c). The excitation-dependent behavior of carbon dots might be due to the non-uniform size of the CDs and the emission or absorption of energy influenced by the surface functional groups instead of the core lattice structure of CDs. In addition to the optical properties, the quantum yield of N-CDs was also evaluated using a spectrophotometer and the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs was measured and displayed its Time-resolved photoluminescence spectrum in Fig. 4c. The average fluorescence lifetime (τ avg) of N-CDs was found at 7.1 ns with a faster (τ1, τ1) and slower (τ3) decay constants of 0.39, 2.48 and 11.48 ns, respectively.

Selectivity was performed to determine the N-CDs sensitivity against various metal ions (Fe(II), Cd(II), Co(II), Fe(III), Cr(III), Zn(II), Al(III), Mn(VII), Bisphenol A(BPA), O -phenylenediamine, 2,4-Dinitrophenol(2,4-DNP), phenol, Resorcinol(RS), catechol (CTC), hydroquinone(HQ), o-nitrophenol(o-NP) and p-nitrophenol(p-NP), etc. As indicated in Fig. 6 , only N-CDs showed a discernible variation in fluorescence intensity due to the presence of p-NP, even when other analytes were present. This illustrates that the synthesized N-CDs displayed good selectivity against p-NP in the presence of other analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism for the emission quenching of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed in the previous section, the N-CD fluorescence emission steadily decreased as the pollutant concentration of p-NP increased. To explain the reason behind this observed phenomenon of N-CDs, optical properties and emission quencher were considered and explained. As indicated in Fig.S2a, p-NP displays its maximum absorbance at 400 nm. Similarly, the excitation & and emission spectra of N-CDs are falling at 390 and 455 nm, respectively (Fig. S2 b). Spectral evidence illustrates that the optical spectrum of both quencher and N-CDs overlap in a similar spectral region. This observation is well-matched with the Inner Filter Effect (IFE). This is due to the reabsorption of emitted energy and absorption of the incident radiation by a chromophore other than the proposed primary absorber [59]. Usually, this occurs whenever the quencher absorption maxima overlap with the fluorescent materials' emission or excitation energy. However, this does not explain or support the quenching but rather elucidates the deterioration of the emission or excitation energy of N-CDs with a gradual rise in the concentration of p-NP [60]. Hence, IFE might be the major reason for the emission intensity decrease in N-CDs [42].

We investigated the practicality of the N-CDs potential in sensing the p-NP detection in real samples such as circulating and river water samples. The solutions were prepared, agitated for 5 min, and then incubated for 10 min under ideal circumstances. The fluorescence emission changes and the calculated % recovery and % RSD were recorded. (Relative Standard Deviation) was indicated in Table 2 . The observed results declared that the developed strategy acquired promising accuracy and precision within the acceptable range. This shows how the synthesized CDs are useful for detecting p-NP in real-time/actual samples.

Every five days, the emission intensity produced by N-CDs was measured to assess their photostability at room or ambient temperature. Fig.S2a demonstrates N-CDs' stability at ambient temperature, where the emission intensity was not changed significantly even after 45 days. This illustrates that N-CDs displayed promising stability over a long time. Also, the emission intensity was evaluated at −4 °C. For this, the N-CDs solution was kept at a lower temperature for 15 mins and then recorded the emission intensity. When compared to the N-CDs emission intensity at room temperature, the increased intensity was observed at −4 °C (Fig. S2b). This might be due to the lesser aggregation at lower temperatures than at room temperature; the lower aggregation leads to the higher absorption of source radiation and emitting the high emission intensity. Additionally, the influence of pH was examined to compare pH influence to N-CD emission intensity (Fig.S2c). The fluorescence emission declined with the increasing pH condition from 2 to 8. The increase in emission at lower pH conditions might be due to the protonation under alkaline conditions and the decline in its intensity due to the deprotonation under alkaline conditions. The energy levels broadening due to the OH groups coupling triggered by H-bonding results in rising N-CDs conformational rigidity, thus leading to higher emission intensity at lower pH conditions. At higher pH levels, the loss of the hydrogen bond caused a reduction in the vibrational coupling of OH bonds, which in turn triggered the coupling of the OH groups. This further resulted in the development of discrete energy levels. This circumstance led to lower N-CD emission intensity under higher pH conditions [61]. The ionic strength of N-CDs was evaluated at a concentration range of 10–90 mM. However, NaCl concentration couldn't influence the emission intensity, and no observable emission changes were recorded (Fig.S2d). The current investigation elucidates that the synthesized N-CDs showed promising stability against all investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solvent polarity on fluorescence emission of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The emission intensity changes were recorded in various solvents, which have different polarities (Fig.S3). When N-CDs were added to water, DMF, DMSO, acetone, Chloroform, methanol, and benzene, the N-CDs were completely miscible, but precipitation was observed when N-CDs were in acetic acid and toluene solvents. The polarity of the solvent influenced the solubility of the N-CDs. With increasing polarity, the emission intensity is also increased, and the maximum emission intensity was observed in a water medium. With decreasing polarity, the emission intensity was significantly reduced, and a significant blue shift was observed in the benzene and toluene solvents with lower emission intensity; this might be due to the competition between the inter and intra-molecular hydrogen bonding [62].

This investigation was performed to evaluate whether the produced composites had the catalytic capacity to reduce polluting dyes. Methyl orange was selected for the proposed application as a model azo dye pollutant. Though azo dyes have several industrial applications the major problem is their carcinogenicity for human beings; its high solubility in water supports the migration of dye to all regions of the earth through water. Thus, minimizing the potential harm is a major responsibility while discharging the industrial dye waste into the water bodies.

Catalytic reduction is one of the methods in mineralizing the dye; in this investigation, N-CDs were employed as catalysts for the reduction of MO dye and monitored its reduction potential by recording MO absorbance at 466 nm. In this study, the catalytic reduction of MO dye was carried out using an ice-cooled condition-reducing agent (NaBH4) in the presence and absence of N-CDs (Fig. 8 ). The intensity of MO (Fig. 8a) was shown to decline to a minimum after 135 min, with no significant change in intensity, indicating that total reduction did not occur. However, when the N-CDs (80 μL) were introduced to MO solutions containing sodium borohydride, the intensity of the dyes was significantly reduced (Fig. 8b). Finally, the inclusion of the effective catalyst N-CDs reduced the MO in 8 min. The results confirmed that the N-CDs' catalytic activity increased the reduction rate of MO dyes using NaBH4. The pseudo-first-order kinetics might be used to compute the rate of reaction (Kcat) for the dye reduction of MO by the produced N-CDs (Fig. 8c). The slopes of the linear plots were used to calculate the rate constants (Kcat) and coefficients (R2); the Kcat and R2 values were 0.0208 min−1 and 0.9904, respectively, which illustrates that the synthesized N-CDs are potential catalysts for reducing the harmful chemicals, especially dyes.

This is explained by the well-known Langmuir-Hinshelwood (LH) model. The dye-NaBH4 interaction was a charge transfer process, with the dye molecules functioning as electron acceptors and the NaBH4 molecules acting as electron donors. However, in the absence of (N-CDs), the reduction of dyes was kinetically regulated solely in the presence of NaBH4 due to the substantial potential difference between them. The borohydride (BH4) ion is released and joins on the surface of the N-CDs when the NaBH4 is dissociated. The BH4 ion and the surface of the N-CDs will create a covalent bond. Similarly, MO dye could be added to the surface of N-CDs. The nucleophile BH4 ions subsequently give electrons to dye molecules via the electron mediator (N-CDs). After absorbing electrons from catalysts and H2 from hydride (BH4) ions, the dye molecules were reduced and subsequently desorbed from the N-CDs, allowing the reduction reaction to continue. In addition, a substantial amount of H2 gas was created during the reduction reaction. As a result, the extraordinary catalytic reduction activity of produced N-CDs was attributed to the dye molecules' enormous active surface area and effective electron-accepting potential.

It demonstrates that the methyl orange reduction process with artificial N-CDs was both kinetically and thermodynamically advantageous. It was discovered that the green catalyst lowered the activation energy and increased the rate of reaction [63]. The schematic representations of the methyl orange reduction are displayed in Scheme 1 .

The bactericidal potential of N-CDs against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria was evaluated primarily through the well-diffusion method. For comparison purposes, the well-diffusion was performed without inoculating N-CDs. Fig. 9 represents the inhibition zones of the N-CDs in opposition to L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, E. coli, and S. enterica bacteria and control as a reference. As per the experimental observation, with the addition of 100 μL N-CDs (300 μg) and incubation, bacterial growth inhibition was observed around the well against all the bacterial strains. The inhibition zone areas were tabulated in Table 3 , where the inhibition zone area was found to be similar for three bacteria, which are gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, except for E. coli. However, the microdilution method was utilized to evaluate N-CDs bactericidal potential further. Table 3 shows the MIC and MBC values of N-CDs against pathogens (∼106 CFU/mL). The N-CDs displayed similar MIC and MBC (240/470 μg/mL) for L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, and S. enterica. However, it displayed a weaker antibacterial effect against E. coli (470/940 μg/mL), as we observed a low inhibition zone (10 mm) in the well-diffusion method. This illustrates that N-CDs are more effective against gram-positive bacteria than gram-negative. Similar outcomes for CDs with PEG capped have been seen in past research [64]. The most probable reason for this pattern might be the difference in the cell wall structure between gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. This increased activity also suggests that there may be more mechanisms influencing the antibacterial activity of N-CDs than bacterial cell wall construction, and further research is needed in this area. Taking this result as an advantage for developing the synthesized N-CDs as an antibacterial spray against bacterial strains for commercial and therapeutic uses with further development.

Here, we provided a straightforward procedure for producing N-CDs by the hydrothermal method. The synthesized material displayed multi-functionality as a fluorescence sensor, catalyst, and antibacterial agent. The detection experiment revealed N-CDs' potential against p-NP by displaying the lower detection limit of 20 nm with promising selectivity. The mechanism behind selective detection was also explained through the IFE mechanism. Even the river and circulating water samples environment displayed good precision and accuracy. As a catalyst, it proved its potential in reducing the methyl orange dye in 8 min with a promising rate constant. Further, the antimicrobial activity against gram-positive and gram-negative also proved the bactericidal efficiency of N-CDs by the well-diffusion and microdilution method (MIC: 235–470 μg/mL) (MBC: 470–940 μg/mL). Further research is necessary to find the reason behind the weak antimicrobial activity of N-CDs against gram-negative bacteria, i.e., E. coli. Based on the findings mentioned above and analysis, we can conclude that the suggested N-CDs can function as a promising water pollutant sensor and a bactericidal agent for gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria and can be used, after further development, as a well-known pollutant sensor and bactericidal agent. Usually, the functional surface of any nanomaterials could influence the functional properties of the material, and this current synthesis method could influence at the surface level, bringing the emission from the core lattice structure of carbon dots or influencing the core lattice structure may further improve the properties of carbon dots, which would further influence the quantum yield of the material. Not only the detoxification, further mineralization of the pollutant is also necessary within in very short time, irrespective of the pollutant's chemical structure. Also, the strong photostability is the best positive aspect with promising antibacterial activity. However, the reported methods need to be tested against several common viral pathogens, bacteria and fungal moieties to make it a better antipathogenic spray.

Sai Kumar Tammina: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jyothi L.: Investigation. Jothi Vinoth Kumar: Investigation. Harshal Srivastava: Investigation. Sri Ram G. Naraharisetty: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from DST-SERB (EMR/000516), DST-Purse (SoP, UoH), Respond Project ISRO (ISRO/RES/3/799/18-19), and UoH Institute of Eminence (IoE) grant (RC1-20-011).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111013.
